findings.location: Section 3 — Bayesian Updating from Sightings, opening of 'The action-gate architecture is the load-bearing innovation for dogs' paragraph: 'An adversarial search for counterexamples — situations where crowd response to sightings of fearful dogs actually succeeded — returned zero.'
findings.issue: The draft asserts zero counterexamples to the 'crowd response is fatal' rule, but the vault's own corpus repeatedly cites Weiss 2012 (49% of recoveries via neighborhood search) and Lord 2007 (35% via shelter call/visit + 15% posted signs) — both of which involve broad community broadcast and group involvement. Sources that 'broadcast widely' (PetPlace, Lord 2007 ScienceDaily) explicitly frame social media and community broadcast as the *second* most common recovery channel. The draft suppresses this counter-stream by reframing all such cases retroactively as 'gregarious-dominated'. That reframing is the draft's own hypothesis, not an empirical finding — yet it is presented as a closed adversarial search.
findings.recommendation: Replace the absolute 'returned zero' phrasing with a more honest framing: the adversarial search for *fearful-dog* crowd-success cases returned zero, but neighborhood/community broadcast is the dominant recovery channel for the (gregarious-dominated) general population per Weiss 2012 and Lord 2007. Acknowledge that the 'all crowd response is fatal' rule is conditional on temperament classification, and that misclassifying a gregarious dog as fearful imposes a non-trivial cost (foregone neighborhood-search recovery) — not the 'only wasted trap time' the draft elsewhere claims.

findings.location: Executive Summary, sentence: 'while misclassifying a gregarious dog as fearful only wastes trap time.'
findings.issue: This is the load-bearing asymmetry claim of the entire architecture, and it is overstated. The vault evidence (Weiss 2012: 49% neighborhood search; Lord 2007: shelter call/signs dominate) shows that for gregarious dogs, fast active neighborhood broadcast is the primary recovery method. Defaulting to passive camera-station-only for a gregarious dog does not 'only waste trap time' — it forgoes the empirically dominant recovery channel and may extend time-to-recovery enough that the dog crosses the 72h Phase 2 threshold the draft itself defines. The asymmetric-cost argument is structurally correct but the magnitude claim on the false-fearful side is wrong.
findings.recommendation: Qualify the asymmetry claim. Suggested insertion after 'only wastes trap time': add a clause acknowledging that misclassifying a gregarious dog as fearful also forgoes the neighborhood-search channel that produces ~49% of all recoveries (Weiss 2012) and can push the dog past the 72h cooperative window into actual Phase 2 — so the asymmetry, while real, is not 'fatal vs. costless' but 'fatal vs. materially-suboptimal'. This honesty strengthens, not weakens, the architectural argument.

findings.location: Section 2 — Breed-Specific Behavioral Priors, 'Knowledge Gaps' paragraph and Calibration table row 'Galgo stranger-approach success, day 1 | 0.02 | high (5+ practitioner consensus)'.
findings.issue: The draft assigns 'high' confidence to a numerical day-1 stranger-approach success rate of 0.02 for galgos, attributed to '5+ practitioner consensus'. The vault does NOT contain any source giving a numerical probability; Normando 2024's actual finding is '>1 in 5 galgos show stranger-fear' (a population-level prevalence, not an individual day-1 approach success rate). Inventing a 0.02 number with 'high' confidence is exactly the over-precision the Knowledge Gaps section warns against ('Normando 2024 does not provide numerical prevalence beyond >1 in 5'). This is a fabrication risk that will be encoded directly into the system prompt.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) downgrade the confidence on the 0.02 figure to 'low — analyst inference, no empirical source', or (b) replace the numerical row with a qualitative entry ('Galgo stranger-approach default: forbidden, no empirical day-1 success rate published') and explicitly note in Knowledge Gaps that the 0.02 figure used in earlier versions was a synthesizer inference not grounded in any specific source. The action gate doesn't actually need the number — only the qualitative 'passive-only' rule. Encoding a false-precision number into priors will produce miscalibrated downstream Bayes updates.

findings.location: Section 2 — Breed-Specific Behavioral Priors, Per-Category Operational Table row 'Podenco | 5–15 km (prey-drive); 2–5 km (default)' and Calibration table 'Podenco lost-radius 24h (prey-drive escape) | 5–10 km | medium'.
findings.issue: The podenco radius figures are presented in calibration tables with 'medium' confidence, but the depth-investigation interim explicitly states they are 'mechanistically derived' from McLennan PMS + endurance physiology + scrubland ecology with ZERO GPS telemetry data. The interim's own confidence assessment is 70%, with the explicit caveat that 'A GPS telemetry dataset of 20+ lost hunting dogs in Mediterranean scrubland terrain showing median displacement under 2 km in 48 hours would invalidate the large-radius encoding.' The draft passes through the numbers without that explicit invalidation condition. A user encoding 5–15 km into a search-area prior will deploy resources across a 700+ km² area on the basis of inference, not measurement.
findings.recommendation: Insert a one-sentence caveat in Section 2's Knowledge Gaps subsection explicitly stating: 'The podenco 5–15 km radius is mechanistic inference from predatory-motor + endurance + ecology; zero lost-podenco GPS telemetry exists. If Nona's first ~20 recovered podencos show median displacement <2 km, the encoded prior must be revised downward.' Also flag in Practical Encoding that the podenco radius should be treated as a parameter to refit from operational data, not a hard prior.

findings.location: Section 1 — Temporal Behavioral Phases, paragraph beginning 'The four-stream triangulation describes the *population mean* phase boundary' and the claim 'For gregarious dogs with opportunistic escapes... Phase 1 may stretch to 5–7 days'.
findings.issue: The draft frames the bimodal-mixture finding as if Phase 1 can stretch to 5–7 days for gregarious dogs, but the calibration table encodes Phase 1 default duration as 0–72h. The phase classifier code uses 72h as the cap for gregarious + opportunistic. The narrative and the encoded rule contradict each other. Lord 2007's 2-day median actually supports the shorter phase boundary; the 5–7 day extension is speculative and not separately sourced. This is an internal inconsistency a system-prompt encoder must resolve.
findings.recommendation: Reconcile the two passages. Either (a) extend the gregarious + opportunistic Phase 1 cap in the pseudocode to 120h or 168h to match the narrative, or (b) tighten the narrative to say Phase 1 cooperation *behavior* can persist past the formal phase boundary but the engine treats 72h as the trigger to escalate caution. Option (b) is cleaner. Add one sentence acknowledging the tension explicitly.

findings.location: Section 3 — Bayesian Updating from Sightings, paragraph 'The Lord 2007 vs. MAR-doctrine tension resolves through temperament-gating' and 'Nona disproportionately encounters the hard-case population because easy cases resolve before platform engagement.'
findings.issue: This survivorship-bias argument is asserted but never tested against evidence. The draft treats it as the resolution that justifies defaulting to MAR-doctrine passive protocols for *all* Nona cases. But the dialectic gap is: Red Cão Algarve is a *municipal* and *community-facing* rescue platform, not a specialist MAR consultancy. There is no evidence in the vault that easy gregarious cases will resolve before platform engagement — the platform is positioned as the first call for any lost-dog event, including the easy ones. If the platform's actual case mix is closer to Weiss 2012 than to MAR's hard-case archive, then defaulting to passive-only protocols across the board will reduce recovery rates for the gregarious majority.
findings.recommendation: Add a paragraph acknowledging that the survivorship-bias resolution assumes Nona's case mix is dominated by hard cases — and that this is an assumption, not an established fact. Recommend that the first ~100 Nona cases be logged with intake temperament + outcome to validate the case-mix assumption. If actual case mix is gregarious-dominated, the action-gate defaults need to flip for gregarious + Phase 1. This is the highest-stakes architectural assumption in the report and currently has no exit ramp.

findings.location: Section 4 — Fear Escalation Dynamics, 'olfactory shutdown' paragraph: 'the olfactory portion of a dog's brain closes down during the fight or flight process'.
findings.issue: The draft already partially hedges this claim ('Biologically plausible... though the specific shutdown claim is not directly supported by canine neuroimaging in this corpus'), but then immediately uses olfactory shutdown as a justification for why food lures fail at peak arousal — coupling a hedged claim to a load-bearing protocol rule. The MAR source ('What You Don't Know About Lost Pets') is a practitioner article, not a neuroscience study. There is a simpler explanation (sympathetic-mediated reduction in appetitive motivation; food is non-salient under high arousal) that does not require an olfactory-shutdown mechanism.
findings.recommendation: Reword to make the operational rule (food lures fail during peak arousal) the primary claim, with both candidate mechanisms (olfactory shutdown + appetitive-motivation suppression) listed as alternative hypotheses. The rule survives regardless of which mechanism is correct; the draft should not anchor the rule to the weaker hypothesis.

findings.location: Section 2 — Breed-Specific Behavioral Priors, 'Practical Encoding' Layer 1 action-gate block: 'galgo: passive_only + conspecific_lure + camera_first + no_crowd_response'.
findings.issue: The draft's galgo prior is a single homogeneous protocol, but Section 2's own analysis distinguishes two galgo sub-populations: trauma-baseline hunting-discarded galgos (Normando's sample) and rescued-as-pet galgos. The draft acknowledges in Knowledge Gaps that 'rescued-as-pet galgos likely have lower stranger-fear priors than hunting-discarded individuals, but the local mix is unknown'. Yet the encoded action gate applies the maximum-trauma protocol to all galgos uniformly. For a galgo that has been a family pet for 5 years and slipped a leash on a beach walk, requiring 72h camera-first deployment is a structural mismatch with the actual case profile.
findings.recommendation: Add a sub-classifier for galgos: 'galgo with ≥12 months stable home + no prior fearful sighting history' should drop to the sighthound_other protocol (passive-first but with shorter camera-first window and conditional approach if signals support). The fully passive 72h+ protocol should apply only to galgos with unknown history, recent rescue, or any documented stranger-fear or trauma signal. This is a one-table change in the action-gate code and the table row should be added explicitly.

findings.location: Section 4 — Fear Escalation Dynamics, Calibration table row 'Name-conditioning reversibility | unknown — assume permanent | medium'.
findings.issue: The 'assume permanent' default is operationally safe but contradicts the draft's own Lacey-Elvis case description, which shows owner-unique non-conditioned signal substitution worked. The draft acknowledges this in narrative ('partial reversibility via non-conditioned signal substitution') but the calibration row encodes 'permanent'. A user reading only the table will not learn that the signal-substitution workaround exists.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite the row as 'Name-conditioning: assume permanent for *name*; non-name unique owner signals (singing, unique whistle pattern) may bypass conditioning per Lacey case'. This preserves the safe default while making the workaround discoverable from the table alone.

findings.location: Executive Summary, sentence 'breed governs the gate at full weight even though breed explains only ~9% of individual behavioral variance'.
findings.issue: This phrasing papers over the deepest tension in the report. Morrill 2022 shows breed is *least* predictive on the agonistic-threshold dimension — which is exactly the dimension governing flight behavior. The draft resolves this by switching frames (individual prediction vs. group mean) but the executive summary reader gets the easy version without the caveat. A reader who acts on the executive summary alone will encode 'breed = 100% of gate weight' without absorbing the conditional 'until individual signals arrive' clause. This is the single most consequential claim for system-prompt encoding.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite the executive-summary sentence to surface the conditional. Suggested: 'breed governs the gate at initialization (when no individual signals are available) but degrades quickly as individual sighting evidence arrives; on the agonistic-threshold dimension specifically, Morrill 2022 shows breed is least predictive, so individual evidence should override the breed prior more aggressively than for other behavioral dimensions.' This single sentence is the difference between a defensible system-prompt encoding and a stereotyping one.

findings.location: Section 3 — Bayesian Updating from Sightings, Calibration table λ-weight rows (0.95 camera, 0.70 daylight eyewitness, etc.) and the disclaimer that these are 'reasoned from adjacent domains, not measured'.
findings.issue: The λ weights are presented with two-decimal precision in a calibration table that mixes high-confidence measured anchors (Lord 2007 median, Kremer 91%) with reasoned-not-measured λ values. A casual reader (or a system-prompt encoder) will treat all table entries as equally calibrated. The Knowledge Gaps disclaimer is two paragraphs away and easily missed.
findings.recommendation: Either add a confidence column to the λ rows explicitly marking them 'inference, refit from operational data' or prefix the λ block with one inline sentence: 'The λ weights below are starting points reasoned from adjacent-domain literature; they MUST be refit once Nona has 50+ cases with sighting-quality × outcome pairs.' The numeric precision should not survive in the table without that qualifier directly adjacent.

findings.location: Section 4 — Fear Escalation Dynamics, Decision matrix table, row 'Gregarious | Phase 1 | Owner direct approach + neighborhood search OK; broadcast permitted'.
findings.issue: This row contradicts the Section 3 action-gate code, which defaults to 'private_coordinator_only + responder_count=1' unless temperament=gregarious AND phase=phase_1_acute AND sighting.minutes_ago<30 AND sighting.dog_stationary_with_finder. The matrix says 'broadcast permitted' broadly for gregarious + Phase 1; the code says broadcast is permitted only under four conjoint conditions. These are different recommendations encoded in the same report. The matrix is what a human owner reads; the code is what the engine runs. Mismatch is dangerous.
findings.evidence: Internal cross-reference between Section 3 (action_gate code) and Section 4 (decision matrix)
findings.recommendation: Edit the decision-matrix row to match the code: 'Gregarious | Phase 1 | Owner direct approach OK; semi-public calm-protocol broadcast permitted only if sighting <30 min old AND dog stationary with finder; otherwise coordinator-only.' Alignment of narrative and code is non-negotiable for a spec that will be encoded into an AI system prompt.
